revision.comment: [[WP:AES|←]][[官人#職事・散位]]へのリダイレクト
revision.text: #転送 [[官人#職事・散位]]

'''散位'''（さんい/さんに）とは、[[日本]]の[[律令制]]において、[[京官|内]][[外官|外]]の[[官司]]に執掌を持たず、[[位階]]のみを持つ者。
なお、'''[[散官]]'''（さんかん）という別称もある。[[唐]]にも散官の制度があり、文官には光禄大夫などの文散官、武官には鎮軍大将軍などの武散官が与えられていたが、[[品階]]を有する全ての官人に与えられる性格のものであり、実態としては日本における位階に近い。他の別称としては、[[女嬬]]・[[采女]]などの下級[[女官]]や地方の[[国衙]]に仕えた[[雑任]]などを指す'''散事'''（さんじ）や、同じく地方の雑任を指した'''散仕'''（さんじ）がある。
== 概要 == 律令制において官司の数やその職員の定員に制約がある以上、職事官あるいは[[官位相当制]]に相応しい官職を全ての官人に与えることは困難であり、特に[[蔭子]]・[[位子]]による優遇が大きかった日本では散位の者が発生することがあらかじめ予想されていた。
例えば、蔭子・位子の中で執掌が与えられていない者、[[致仕]]や行政整理による廃官によって執掌を失った者、位階の昇進によって次の執掌を与えられるまで散位に編入される者、病気や服喪によって[[解官]]された者、[[白丁]]・无位（無位）から[[考]]によって位階を授けられたものの執掌が与えられていない者、財物などを献上して[[叙位]]された者などを指す。
在京の五位以上は[[散位寮]]に[[長上]]し、六位以下は在京の者は散位寮に[[分番]]で仕え、地方在住者は現地の国衙に分番で仕えた。彼らは[[式部省]]の[[補任]]によって散位身分のまま[[兼帯]]する形で[[造寺司]]などの令制外の機関職員に派遣されたり、[[国司]]の補任によって国衙の[[目代]]や[[在庁官人]]、雑任に補任されたり、その他様々な形態で内外の官司に派遣されて雑務に従事した。後に散位の増加に対応するために定額（定員）を定め、定額から外れた者は出仕の代わりに[[続労銭]]を納めて勤務実績（[[労]]）の代替とした。
なお、「才職不相当（職に見合うだけの才能に持っていない場合）」（[[選叙令]]散位条）に散位に配される場合もあり、本来は自称する性格のものではなかった。だが、本来は[[世襲]]の地位であり、官位相当制の対象外であった[[郡司]]においては位階に叙せられること、すなわち散位は[[譜第]]としての性格や課役免除の特権の存在など、一般の農民との違いを示す重要な指標となり得た。そのため、郡司や[[刀禰]]など在地の有力者が位階を得て文書において「散位」と[[位署]]することが広く行われていた。
== 参考文献 == *山田英雄「散位」（『国史大辞典 6』（吉川弘文館、1985年） ISBN 978-4-642-00505-0） *山田英雄「散位」（『日本史大事典 3』（平凡社、1993年） ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1） *堀井典子「散位」（『日本古代史事典』（朝倉書店、2005年） ISBN 978-4-254-53014-8） *梅村喬「古代官職制と〈職〉」『「職」成立過程の研究』（校倉書房、2011年） ISBN 978-4-7517-4360-7
{{DEFAULTSORT:さんい}} [[Category:日本の律令制]]

なお、「才職不相当（職に見合うだけの才能に持っていない場合）」（[[選叙令]]散位条）に散位に配される場合もあり、本来は自称する性格のものではなかった。だが、本来は[[世襲]]の地位であり、官位相当制の対象外であった[[郡司]]においては位階に叙せられること、すなわち散位は[[譜第]]としての性格や課役免除の特権の存在など、一般の農民との違いを示す重要な指標となり得た。そのため、郡司や[[刀禰]]など在地の有力者が位階を得て文書において「散位」と[[位署]]することが広く行われていた。

在京の五位以上は[[散位寮]]に[[長上]]し、六位以下は在京の者は散位寮に[[分番]]で仕え、地方在住者は現地の国衙に分番で仕えた。彼らは[[式部省]]の[[補任]]によって散位身分のまま[[兼帯]]する形で[[造寺司]]などの令制外の機関職員に派遣されたり、[[国司]]の補任によって国衙の[[目代]]や[[在庁官人]]、雑任に補任されたり、その他様々な形態で内外の官司に派遣されて雑務に従事した。後に散位の増加に対応するために定額（定員）を定め、定額から外れた者は出仕の代わりに[[続労銭]]を納めて勤務実績（[[労]]）の代替とした。

== 参考文献 == *山田英雄「散位」（『国史大辞典 6』（吉川弘文館、1985年） ISBN 978-4-642-00505-0） *山田英雄「散位」（『日本史大事典 3』（平凡社、1993年） ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1） *堀井典子「散位」（『日本古代史事典』（朝倉書店、2005年） ISBN 978-4-254-53014-8） *[[梅村喬]]「古代官職制と〈職〉」『「職」成立過程の研究』（校倉書房、2011年） ISBN 978-4-7517-4360-7
{{DEFAULTSORT:さんい}} [[Category:日本の律令制]]

'''散位'''（さんい/さんに）とは、[[日本]]の[[律令制]]において、[[京官|内]][[外官|外]]の[[官司]]に執掌を持たず、[[位階]]のみを持つ者。なお、'''[[散官]]'''（さんかん）という別称もある。[[唐]]にも散官の制度があり、文官には光禄大夫などの文散官、武官には鎮軍大将軍などの武散官が与えられていたが、[[品階]]を有する全ての官人に与えられる性格のものであり、実態としては日本における位階に近い。他の別称としては、[[女嬬]]・[[采女]]などの下級[[女官]]や地方の[[国衙]]に仕えた[[雑任]]などを指す'''散事'''（さんじ）や、同じく地方の雑任を指した'''散仕'''（さんじ）がある。
== 概要 == 律令制において官司の数やその職員の定員に制約がある以上、職事官あるいは[[官位相当制]]に相応しい官職を全ての官人に与えることは困難であり、特に[[蔭子]]・[[位子]]による優遇が大きかった日本では散位の者が発生することが予想されていた。
例えば、蔭子・位子の中で執掌が与えられていない者、[[致仕]]や行政整理による廃官によって執掌を失った者、位階の昇進によって次の執掌を与えられるまで散位に編入される者、病気や服喪によって[[解官]]された者、[[白丁]]・[[无位]]（無位）から[[考]]によって位階を授けられたものの執掌が与えられていない者、財物などを献上して[[叙位]]された者などを指す。
在京の五位以上は[[散位寮]]に[[長上]]し、六位以下は在京の者は散位寮に[[分番]]で仕え、地方在住者は現地の国衙に分番で仕えた。彼らは[[式部省]]の[[補任]]によって散位身分のまま[[兼帯]]する形で[[造寺司]]などの令制外の機関職員に派遣されたり、[[国司]]の補任によって国衙の[[目代]]や[[在庁官人]]、雑任に補任されたり、その他様々な形態で内外の官司に派遣されて雑務に従事した。後に散位の増加に対応するために定額（定員）を定め、定額から外れた者は出仕の代わりに[[続労銭]]を納めて勤務実績（[[労]]）の代替とした。
なお、「才職不相当（職に見合うだけの才能に持っていない場合）」（[[選叙令]]散位条）に散位に配される場合もあり、本来は自称する性格のものではなかった。だが、[[世襲]]の地位であり、官位相当制の対象外であった[[郡司]]においては位階に叙せられること、すなわち散位は[[譜第]]としての性格や課役免除の特権の存在など、一般の農民との違いを示す重要な指標となり得た。そのため、郡司や[[刀禰]]など在地の有力者が位階を得て文書において「散位」と[[位署]]することが広く行われていた。
== 参考文献 == *山田英雄「散位」（『国史大辞典 6』（吉川弘文館、1985年） ISBN 978-4-642-00505-0） *山田英雄「散位」（『日本史大事典 3』（平凡社、1993年） ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1） *堀井典子「散位」（『日本古代史事典』（朝倉書店、2005年） ISBN 978-4-254-53014-8） *[[梅村喬]]「古代官職制と〈職〉」『「職」成立過程の研究』（校倉書房、2011年） ISBN 978-4-7517-4360-7
{{DEFAULTSORT:さんい}} [[Category:日本の律令制]]

例えば、蔭子・位子の中で執掌が与えられていない者、[[致仕]]や行政整理による廃官によって執掌を失った者、位階の昇進によって次の執掌を与えられるまで散位に編入される者、病気や服喪によって[[解官]]された者、[[白丁]]・[[无位]]（無位）から[[考]]によって位階を授けられたものの執掌が与えられていない者、財物などを献上して[[叙位]]された者などを指す。

revision.comment: Created page with "'''San'i''' (散位) refers to individuals under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} of {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}} who held only a {{ill|court rank|ja|位階}} without an associated post in either {{ill|central ministries|ja|京官}} or {{ill|provincial offices|ja|外官}}. They were also called '''{{ill|Sankan|ja|散官}}'''. A similar system of "scattered officials" existed in {{ill|Tang China|ja|唐}}, where civil officials were granted honorary positions such as {..."
revision.text:
'''San'i''' (散位) refers to individuals under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} of {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}} who held only a {{ill|court rank|ja|位階}} without an associated post in either {{ill|central ministries|ja|京官}} or {{ill|provincial offices|ja|外官}}. They were also called '''{{ill|Sankan|ja|散官}}'''. A similar system of "scattered officials" existed in {{ill|Tang China|ja|唐}}, where civil officials were granted honorary positions such as {{ill|Guanglu Daifu|ja|光禄大夫}} and military officials were given ranks like {{ill|Zhenjun Da Jiangjun|ja|鎮軍大将軍}}. However, these were honorary ranks conferred on all officials with {{ill|Pin ranks|ja|品階}}, functioning similarly to Japan’s court ranks. Other related terms include '''{{ill|Sanji|ja|散事}}''', referring to lower-ranking {{ill|court ladies|ja|女官}} such as {{ill|Nyoshu|ja|女嬬}} and {{ill|Uneme|ja|采女}}, as well as to provincial minor officials called '''{{ill|Sanshi|ja|散仕}}'''.
== Overview ==
Given the limitations on the number of official posts and staff quotas in the Ritsuryō system, it was impossible to assign posts to all officials in accordance with the {{ill|official rank correspondence system|ja|官位相当制}}. This issue was particularly pronounced in Japan, where the systems of {{ill|Onshi|ja|蔭子}} (privilege based on lineage) and {{ill|Ishi|ja|位子}} (court rank inheritance) created an excess of ranked individuals, many of whom became San'i.
San'i status encompassed various scenarios, including: - Individuals with court ranks from the {{ill|Onshi|ja|蔭子}} or Ishi systems who lacked official appointments. - Those who lost their positions due to {{ill|retirement|ja|致仕}} or administrative restructuring. - Those awaiting reappointment after rank promotion. - Individuals temporarily relieved of duties due to illness or mourning. - Newly ranked persons without appointments, including those elevated from {{ill|Hakuchō|ja|白丁}} (commoners) or {{ill|Mubetsu|ja|无位}} (rankless individuals) through evaluations. - Those who gained ranks through tribute or monetary offerings.
Higher-ranking San'i (fifth rank and above) stationed in the capital were assigned to the {{ill|San'i Bureau|ja|散位寮}}, while those of lower rank (sixth rank and below) either served at the San'i Bureau on a rotational basis if residing in the capital or at local {{ill|provincial offices|ja|国衙}} if based in the provinces. San'i could also receive secondary appointments to various offices, including {{ill|Ritsuryō-external institutions|ja|造寺司}}, or work under provincial governors as deputies ({{ill|Mokudai|ja|目代}}) or lower-ranking officials ({{ill|Zaichō Kanjin|ja|在庁官人}}). To address the growing number of San'i, a fixed quota system was introduced. Those exceeding the quota paid {{ill|Zokurō Coins|ja|続労銭}} to substitute for work obligations.
San'i status could also result from inadequate qualifications for a specific role, as prescribed under the {{ill|Senjo-ryō|ja|選叙令}} (Selection and Appointment Ordinance). Although not inherently a title for self-identification, it became a significant marker of status for local elites such as {{ill|Gunji|ja|郡司}} or {{ill|Tōne|ja|刀禰}}, who used their San'i rank in documentation to distinguish themselves from commoners and highlight their privileges, including tax exemptions.
== References == * Yamada Hideo, "San'i," in {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典}} 6 (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985), ISBN 978-4-642-00505-0. * Yamada Hideo, "San'i," in {{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten|ja|日本史大事典}} 3 (Heibonsha, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1. * Hori Noriko, "San'i," in {{ill|Nihon Kodai-shi Jiten|ja|日本古代史事典}} (Asakura Shoten, 2005), ISBN 978-4-254-53014-8. * Uemura Takashi, "Ancient Official Systems and the Role of 'Shoku'," in *Studies on the Formation of 'Shoku'* (Kōzō Shoin, 2011), ISBN 978-4-7517-4360-7.
[[Category:Ritsuryō System]] [[ja:散位]]

They were also called '''{{ill|Sankan|ja|散官}}'''. A similar system of "scattered officials" existed in {{ill|Tang China|ja|唐}}, where civil officials were granted honorary positions such as {{ill|Guanglu Daifu|ja|光禄大夫}} and military officials were given ranks like {{ill|Zhenjun Da Jiangjun|ja|鎮軍大将軍}}. San'i status encompassed various scenarios, including: - Individuals with court ranks from the {{ill|Onshi|ja|蔭子}} or Ishi systems who lacked official appointments. - Those who lost their positions due to {{ill|retirement|ja|致仕}} or administrative restructuring. - Those awaiting reappointment after rank promotion. - Individuals temporarily relieved of duties due to illness or mourning. - Newly ranked persons without appointments, including those elevated from {{ill|Hakuchō|ja|白丁}} (commoners) or {{ill|Mubetsu|ja|无位}} (rankless individuals) through evaluations. - Those who gained ranks through tribute or monetary offerings.

San'i could also receive secondary appointments to various offices, including {{ill|Ritsuryō-external institutions|ja|造寺司}}, or work under provincial governors as deputies ({{ill|Mokudai|ja|目代}}) or lower-ranking officials ({{ill|Zaichō Kanjin|ja|在庁官人}}). To address the growing number of San'i, a fixed quota system was introduced. Those exceeding the quota paid {{ill|Zokurō Coins|ja|続労銭}} to substitute for work obligations.
San'i status could also result from inadequate qualifications for a specific role, as prescribed under the {{ill|Senjo-ryō|ja|選叙令}} (Selection and Appointment Ordinance). Although not inherently a title for self-identification, it became a significant marker of status for local elites such as {{ill|Gunji|ja|郡司}} or {{ill|Tōne|ja|刀禰}}, who used their San'i rank in documentation to distinguish themselves from commoners and highlight their privileges, including tax exemptions.
== References == * Yamada Hideo, "San'i," in {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典}} 6 (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985), ISBN 978-4-642-00505-0. * Yamada Hideo, "San'i," in {{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten|ja|日本史大事典}} 3 (Heibonsha, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1. * Hori Noriko, "San'i," in {{ill|Nihon Kodai-shi Jiten|ja|日本古代史事典}} (Asakura Shoten, 2005), ISBN 978-4-254-53014-8. * Uemura Takashi, "Ancient Official Systems and the Role of 'Shoku'," in *Studies on the Formation of 'Shoku'* (Kōzō Shoin, 2011), ISBN 978-4-7517-4360-7.
[[Category:Ritsuryō System]] [[Category:new pages]][[category:original pages]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:New pages]][[Category:New pages]][[ja:散位]]

'''San'i''' (散位) refers to individuals under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} of {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}} who held only a {{ill|court rank|ja|位階}} without an associated post in either {{ill|central ministries|ja|京官}} or {{ill|provincial offices|ja|外官}}. They were also called '''{{ill|Sankan|ja|散官}}'''. A similar system of "scattered officials" existed in {{ill|Tang China|ja|唐}}, where civil officials were granted honorary positions such as {{ill|Guanglu Daifu|ja|光禄大夫}} and military officials were given ranks like {{ill|Zhenjun Da Jiangjun|ja|鎮軍大将軍}}. However, these were honorary ranks conferred on all officials with {{ill|Pin ranks|ja|品階}}, functioning similarly to Japan’s court ranks. Other related terms include '''{{ill|Sanji|ja|散事}}''', referring to lower-ranking {{ill|court ladies|ja|女官}} such as {{ill|Nyoshu|ja|女嬬}} and {{ill|Uneme|ja|采女}}, as well as to provincial minor officials called '''{{ill|Sanshi|ja|散仕}}'''.
== Overview ==
Given the limitations on the number of official posts and staff quotas in the Ritsuryō system, it was impossible to assign posts to all officials in accordance with the {{ill|official rank correspondence system|ja|官位相当制}}. This issue was particularly pronounced in Japan, where the systems of {{ill|Onshi|ja|蔭子}} (privilege based on lineage) and {{ill|Ishi|ja|位子}} (court rank inheritance) created an excess of ranked individuals, many of whom became San'i.
San'i status encompassed various scenarios, including: - Individuals with court ranks from the {{ill|Onshi|ja|蔭子}} or Ishi systems who lacked official appointments. - Those who lost their positions due to {{ill|retirement|ja|致仕}} or administrative restructuring. - Those awaiting reappointment after rank promotion. - Individuals temporarily relieved of duties due to illness or mourning. - Newly ranked persons without appointments, including those elevated from {{ill|Hakuchō|ja|白丁}} (commoners) or {{ill|Mubetsu|ja|无位}} (rankless individuals) through evaluations. - Those who gained ranks through tribute or monetary offerings.
Higher-ranking San'i (fifth rank and above) stationed in the capital were assigned to the {{ill|San'i Bureau|ja|散位寮}}, while those of lower rank (sixth rank and below) either served at the San'i Bureau on a rotational basis if residing in the capital or at local {{ill|provincial offices|ja|国衙}} if based in the provinces. San'i could also receive secondary appointments to various offices, including {{ill|Ritsuryō-external institutions|ja|造寺司}}, or work under provincial governors as deputies ({{ill|Mokudai|ja|目代}}) or lower-ranking officials ({{ill|Zaichō Kanjin|ja|在庁官人}}). To address the growing number of San'i, a fixed quota system was introduced. Those exceeding the quota paid {{ill|Zokurō Coins|ja|続労銭}} to substitute for work obligations.
San'i status could also result from inadequate qualifications for a specific role, as prescribed under the {{ill|Senjo-ryō|ja|選叙令}} (Selection and Appointment Ordinance). Although not inherently a title for self-identification, it became a significant marker of status for local elites such as {{ill|Gunji|ja|郡司}} or {{ill|Tōne|ja|刀禰}}, who used their San'i rank in documentation to distinguish themselves from commoners and highlight their privileges, including tax exemptions.
== References == * Yamada Hideo, "San'i," in {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典}} 6 (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985), ISBN 978-4-642-00505-0. * Yamada Hideo, "San'i," in {{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten|ja|日本史大事典}} 3 (Heibonsha, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1. * Hori Noriko, "San'i," in {{ill|Nihon Kodai-shi Jiten|ja|日本古代史事典}} (Asakura Shoten, 2005), ISBN 978-4-254-53014-8. * Uemura Takashi, "Ancient Official Systems and the Role of 'Shoku'," in *Studies on the Formation of 'Shoku'* (Kōzō Shoin, 2011), ISBN 978-4-7517-4360-7.

== References == * Yamada Hideo, "San'i," in {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典}} 6 (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985), ISBN 978-4-642-00505-0. * Yamada Hideo, "San'i," in {{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten|ja|日本史大事典}} 3 (Heibonsha, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1. * Hori Noriko, "San'i," in {{ill|Nihon Kodai-shi Jiten|ja|日本古代史事典}} (Asakura Shoten, 2005), ISBN 978-4-254-53014-8. * Uemura Takashi, "Ancient Official Systems and the Role of 'Shoku'," in *Studies on the Formation of 'Shoku'* (Kōzō Shoin, 2011), ISBN 978-4-7517-4360-7.
[[Category:Ritsuryō System]] [[category:original pages]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:pp]][[category:San'i]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:pp]][[category:San'i]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:pp]][[category:San'i]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:original pages]][[category:San'i]][[category:original pages]]
[[ja:散位]]

'''San'i''' (散位) refers to individuals under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} of {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}} who held only a {{ill|court rank|ja|位階}} without an associated post in either {{ill|central ministries|ja|京官}} or {{ill|provincial offices|ja|外官}}. They were also called '''{{ill|Sankan|ja|散官}}'''. A similar system of "scattered officials" existed in {{ill|Tang China|ja|唐}}, where civil officials were granted honorary positions such as {{ill|Guanglu Daifu|ja|光禄大夫}} and military officials were given ranks like {{ill|Zhenjun Da Jiangjun|ja|鎮軍大将軍}}. However, these were honorary ranks conferred on all officials with {{ill|Pin ranks|ja|品階}}, functioning similarly to Japan’s court ranks. Other related terms include '''{{ill|Sanji|ja|散事}}''', referring to lower-ranking {{ill|court ladies|ja|女官}} such as {{ill|Nyoshu|ja|女嬬}} and {{ill|Uneme|ja|采女}}, as well as to provincial minor officials called '''{{ill|Sanshi|ja|散仕}}'''.
== Overview ==
Given the limitations on the number of official posts and staff quotas in the Ritsuryō system, it was impossible to assign posts to all officials in accordance with the {{ill|official rank correspondence system|ja|官位相当制}}. This issue was particularly pronounced in Japan, where the systems of {{ill|Onshi|ja|蔭子}} (privilege based on lineage) and {{ill|Ishi|ja|位子}} (court rank inheritance) created an excess of ranked individuals, many of whom became San'i.
San'i status encompassed various scenarios, including: - Individuals with court ranks from the {{ill|Onshi|ja|蔭子}} or Ishi systems who lacked official appointments. - Those who lost their positions due to {{ill|retirement|ja|致仕}} or administrative restructuring. - Those awaiting reappointment after rank promotion. - Individuals temporarily relieved of duties due to illness or mourning. - Newly ranked persons without appointments, including those elevated from {{ill|Hakuchō|ja|白丁}} (commoners) or {{ill|Mubetsu|ja|无位}} (rankless individuals) through evaluations. - Those who gained ranks through tribute or monetary offerings.
Higher-ranking San'i (fifth rank and above) stationed in the capital were assigned to the {{ill|San'i Bureau|ja|散位寮}}, while those of lower rank (sixth rank and below) either served at the San'i Bureau on a rotational basis if residing in the capital or at local {{ill|provincial offices|ja|国衙}} if based in the provinces. San'i could also receive secondary appointments to various offices, including {{ill|Ritsuryō-external institutions|ja|造寺司}}, or work under provincial governors as deputies ({{ill|Mokudai|ja|目代}}) or lower-ranking officials ({{ill|Zaichō Kanjin|ja|在庁官人}}). To address the growing number of San'i, a fixed quota system was introduced. Those exceeding the quota paid {{ill|Zokurō Coins|ja|続労銭}} to substitute for work obligations.
San'i status could also result from inadequate qualifications for a specific role, as prescribed under the {{ill|Senjo-ryō|ja|選叙令}} (Selection and Appointment Ordinance). Although not inherently a title for self-identification, it became a significant marker of status for local elites such as {{ill|Gunji|ja|郡司}} or {{ill|Tōne|ja|刀禰}}, who used their San'i rank in documentation to distinguish themselves from commoners and highlight their privileges, including tax exemptions.
== References == * Yamada Hideo, "San'i," in {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典}} 6 (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985), ISBN 978-4-642-00505-0. * Yamada Hideo, "San'i," in {{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten|ja|日本史大事典}} 3 (Heibonsha, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1. * Hori Noriko, "San'i," in {{ill|Nihon Kodai-shi Jiten|ja|日本古代史事典}} (Asakura Shoten, 2005), ISBN 978-4-254-53014-8. * Uemura Takashi, "Ancient Official Systems and the Role of 'Shoku'," in *Studies on the Formation of 'Shoku'* (Kōzō Shoin, 2011), ISBN 978-4-7517-4360-7.

To address the growing number of San'i, a fixed quota system was introduced. Those exceeding the quota paid {{ill|Zokurō Coins|ja|続労銭}} to substitute for work obligations.
San'i status could also result from inadequate qualifications for a specific role, as prescribed under the {{ill|Senjo-ryō|ja|選叙令}} (Selection and Appointment Ordinance). Although not inherently a title for self-identification, it became a significant marker of status for local elites such as {{ill|Gunji|ja|郡司}} or {{ill|Tōne|ja|刀禰}}, who used their San'i rank in documentation to distinguish themselves from commoners and highlight their privileges, including tax exemptions.

[[ja:散位]]

revision.comment: Moving from [[Category:Original pages]] to [[Category:unsorted link categories]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''San'i''' (散位) refers to individuals under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} of {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}} who held only a {{ill|court rank|ja|位階}} without an associated post in either {{ill|central ministries|ja|京官}} or {{ill|provincial offices|ja|外官}}. They were also called '''{{ill|Sankan|ja|散官}}'''. A similar system of "scattered officials" existed in {{ill|Tang China|ja|唐}}, where civil officials were granted honorary positions such as {{ill|Guanglu Daifu|ja|光禄大夫}} and military officials were given ranks like {{ill|Zhenjun Da Jiangjun|ja|鎮軍大将軍}}. However, these were honorary ranks conferred on all officials with {{ill|Pin ranks|ja|品階}}, functioning similarly to Japan’s court ranks. Other related terms include '''{{ill|Sanji|ja|散事}}''', referring to lower-ranking {{ill|court ladies|ja|女官}} such as {{ill|Nyoshu|ja|女嬬}} and {{ill|Uneme|ja|采女}}, as well as to provincial minor officials called '''{{ill|Sanshi|ja|散仕}}'''.
== Overview ==
Given the limitations on the number of official posts and staff quotas in the Ritsuryō system, it was impossible to assign posts to all officials in accordance with the {{ill|official rank correspondence system|ja|官位相当制}}. This issue was particularly pronounced in Japan, where the systems of {{ill|Onshi|ja|蔭子}} (privilege based on lineage) and {{ill|Ishi|ja|位子}} (court rank inheritance) created an excess of ranked individuals, many of whom became San'i.
San'i status encompassed various scenarios, including: - Individuals with court ranks from the {{ill|Onshi|ja|蔭子}} or Ishi systems who lacked official appointments. - Those who lost their positions due to {{ill|retirement|ja|致仕}} or administrative restructuring. - Those awaiting reappointment after rank promotion. - Individuals temporarily relieved of duties due to illness or mourning. - Newly ranked persons without appointments, including those elevated from {{ill|Hakuchō|ja|白丁}} (commoners) or {{ill|Mubetsu|ja|无位}} (rankless individuals) through evaluations. - Those who gained ranks through tribute or monetary offerings.
Higher-ranking San'i (fifth rank and above) stationed in the capital were assigned to the {{ill|San'i Bureau|ja|散位寮}}, while those of lower rank (sixth rank and below) either served at the San'i Bureau on a rotational basis if residing in the capital or at local {{ill|provincial offices|ja|国衙}} if based in the provinces. San'i could also receive secondary appointments to various offices, including {{ill|Ritsuryō-external institutions|ja|造寺司}}, or work under provincial governors as deputies ({{ill|Mokudai|ja|目代}}) or lower-ranking officials ({{ill|Zaichō Kanjin|ja|在庁官人}}). To address the growing number of San'i, a fixed quota system was introduced. Those exceeding the quota paid {{ill|Zokurō Coins|ja|続労銭}} to substitute for work obligations.
San'i status could also result from inadequate qualifications for a specific role, as prescribed under the {{ill|Senjo-ryō|ja|選叙令}} (Selection and Appointment Ordinance). Although not inherently a title for self-identification, it became a significant marker of status for local elites such as {{ill|Gunji|ja|郡司}} or {{ill|Tōne|ja|刀禰}}, who used their San'i rank in documentation to distinguish themselves from commoners and highlight their privileges, including tax exemptions.
== References == * Yamada Hideo, "San'i," in {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典}} 6 (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985), ISBN 978-4-642-00505-0. * Yamada Hideo, "San'i," in {{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten|ja|日本史大事典}} 3 (Heibonsha, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1. * Hori Noriko, "San'i," in {{ill|Nihon Kodai-shi Jiten|ja|日本古代史事典}} (Asakura Shoten, 2005), ISBN 978-4-254-53014-8. * Uemura Takashi, "Ancient Official Systems and the Role of 'Shoku'," in *Studies on the Formation of 'Shoku'* (Kōzō Shoin, 2011), ISBN 978-4-7517-4360-7.

revision.comment: 1 revision imported: import for reasons specified in the description
revision.text:
'''San'i''' (散位) refers to individuals under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} of {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}} who held only a {{ill|court rank|ja|位階}} without an associated post in either {{ill|central ministries|ja|京官}} or {{ill|provincial offices|ja|外官}}. They were also called '''{{ill|Sankan|ja|散官}}'''. A similar system of "scattered officials" existed in {{ill|Tang China|ja|唐}}, where civil officials were granted honorary positions such as {{ill|Guanglu Daifu|ja|光禄大夫}} and military officials were given ranks like {{ill|Zhenjun Da Jiangjun|ja|鎮軍大将軍}}. However, these were honorary ranks conferred on all officials with {{ill|Pin ranks|ja|品階}}, functioning similarly to Japan’s court ranks. Other related terms include '''{{ill|Sanji|ja|散事}}''', referring to lower-ranking {{ill|court ladies|ja|女官}} such as {{ill|Nyoshu|ja|女嬬}} and {{ill|Uneme|ja|采女}}, as well as to provincial minor officials called '''{{ill|Sanshi|ja|散仕}}'''.
== Overview ==
Given the limitations on the number of official posts and staff quotas in the Ritsuryō system, it was impossible to assign posts to all officials in accordance with the {{ill|official rank correspondence system|ja|官位相当制}}. This issue was particularly pronounced in Japan, where the systems of {{ill|Onshi|ja|蔭子}} (privilege based on lineage) and {{ill|Ishi|ja|位子}} (court rank inheritance) created an excess of ranked individuals, many of whom became San'i.
San'i status encompassed various scenarios, including: - Individuals with court ranks from the {{ill|Onshi|ja|蔭子}} or Ishi systems who lacked official appointments. - Those who lost their positions due to {{ill|retirement|ja|致仕}} or administrative restructuring. - Those awaiting reappointment after rank promotion. - Individuals temporarily relieved of duties due to illness or mourning. - Newly ranked persons without appointments, including those elevated from {{ill|Hakuchō|ja|白丁}} (commoners) or {{ill|Mubetsu|ja|无位}} (rankless individuals) through evaluations. - Those who gained ranks through tribute or monetary offerings.
Higher-ranking San'i (fifth rank and above) stationed in the capital were assigned to the {{ill|San'i Bureau|ja|散位寮}}, while those of lower rank (sixth rank and below) either served at the San'i Bureau on a rotational basis if residing in the capital or at local {{ill|provincial offices|ja|国衙}} if based in the provinces. San'i could also receive secondary appointments to various offices, including {{ill|Ritsuryō-external institutions|ja|造寺司}}, or work under provincial governors as deputies ({{ill|Mokudai|ja|目代}}) or lower-ranking officials ({{ill|Zaichō Kanjin|ja|在庁官人}}). To address the growing number of San'i, a fixed quota system was introduced. Those exceeding the quota paid {{ill|Zokurō Coins|ja|続労銭}} to substitute for work obligations.
San'i status could also result from inadequate qualifications for a specific role, as prescribed under the {{ill|Senjo-ryō|ja|選叙令}} (Selection and Appointment Ordinance). Although not inherently a title for self-identification, it became a significant marker of status for local elites such as {{ill|Gunji|ja|郡司}} or {{ill|Tōne|ja|刀禰}}, who used their San'i rank in documentation to distinguish themselves from commoners and highlight their privileges, including tax exemptions.
== References == * Yamada Hideo, "San'i," in {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典}} 6 (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985), ISBN 978-4-642-00505-0. * Yamada Hideo, "San'i," in {{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten|ja|日本史大事典}} 3 (Heibonsha, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1. * Hori Noriko, "San'i," in {{ill|Nihon Kodai-shi Jiten|ja|日本古代史事典}} (Asakura Shoten, 2005), ISBN 978-4-254-53014-8. * Uemura Takashi, "Ancient Official Systems and the Role of 'Shoku'," in *Studies on the Formation of 'Shoku'* (Kōzō Shoin, 2011), ISBN 978-4-7517-4360-7.

Other related terms include '''{{ill|Sanji|ja|散事}}''', referring to lower-ranking {{ill|court ladies|ja|女官}} such as {{ill|Nyoshu|ja|女嬬}} and {{ill|Uneme|ja|采女}}, as well as to provincial minor officials called '''{{ill|Sanshi|ja|散仕}}'''.

Given the limitations on the number of official posts and staff quotas in the Ritsuryō system, it was impossible to assign posts to all officials in accordance with the {{ill|official rank correspondence system|ja|官位相当制}}. This issue was particularly pronounced in Japan, where the systems of {{ill|Onshi|ja|蔭子}} (privilege based on lineage) and {{ill|Ishi|ja|位子}} (court rank inheritance) created an excess of ranked individuals, many of whom became San'i.
San'i status encompassed various scenarios, including: - Individuals with court ranks from the {{ill|Onshi|ja|蔭子}} or Ishi systems who lacked official appointments. - Those who lost their positions due to {{ill|retirement|ja|致仕}} or administrative restructuring. - Those awaiting reappointment after rank promotion. - Individuals temporarily relieved of duties due to illness or mourning. - Newly ranked persons without appointments, including those elevated from {{ill|Hakuchō|ja|白丁}} (commoners) or {{ill|Mubetsu|ja|无位}} (rankless individuals) through evaluations. - Those who gained ranks through tribute or monetary offerings.
Higher-ranking San'i (fifth rank and above) stationed in the capital were assigned to the {{ill|San'i Bureau|ja|散位寮}}, while those of lower rank (sixth rank and below) either served at the San'i Bureau on a rotational basis if residing in the capital or at local {{ill|provincial offices|ja|国衙}} if based in the provinces. San'i could also receive secondary appointments to various offices, including {{ill|Ritsuryō-external institutions|ja|造寺司}}, or work under provincial governors as deputies ({{ill|Mokudai|ja|目代}}) or lower-ranking officials ({{ill|Zaichō Kanjin|ja|在庁官人}}). To address the growing number of San'i, a fixed quota system was introduced. Those exceeding the quota paid {{ill|Zokurō Coins|ja|続労銭}} to substitute for work obligations.
San'i status could also result from inadequate qualifications for a specific role, as prescribed under the {{ill|Senjo-ryō|ja|選叙令}} (Selection and Appointment Ordinance). Although not inherently a title for self-identification, it became a significant marker of status for local elites such as {{ill|Gunji|ja|郡司}} or {{ill|Tōne|ja|刀禰}}, who used their San'i rank in documentation to distinguish themselves from commoners and highlight their privileges, including tax exemptions.
== References == * Yamada Hideo, "San'i," in {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典}} 6 (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985), ISBN 978-4-642-00505-0. * Yamada Hideo, "San'i," in {{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten|ja|日本史大事典}} 3 (Heibonsha, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1. * Hori Noriko, "San'i," in {{ill|Nihon Kodai-shi Jiten|ja|日本古代史事典}} (Asakura Shoten, 2005), ISBN 978-4-254-53014-8. * Uemura Takashi, "Ancient Official Systems and the Role of 'Shoku'," in *Studies on the Formation of 'Shoku'* (Kōzō Shoin, 2011), ISBN 978-4-7517-4360-7.

revision.text:
'''San'i''' (散位) refers to individuals under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} of {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}} who held only a {{ill|court rank|ja|位階}} without an associated post in either {{ill|central ministries|ja|京官}} or {{ill|provincial offices|ja|外官}}. They were also called '''{{ill|Sankan|ja|散官}}'''. A similar system of "scattered officials" existed in {{ill|Tang China|ja|唐}}, where civil officials were granted honorary positions such as {{ill|Guanglu Daifu|ja|光禄大夫}} and military officials were given ranks like {{ill|Zhenjun Da Jiangjun|ja|鎮軍大将軍}}. However, these were honorary ranks conferred on all officials with {{ill|Pin ranks|ja|品階}}, functioning similarly to Japan’s court ranks. Other related terms include '''{{ill|Sanji|ja|散事}}''', referring to lower-ranking {{ill|court ladies|ja|女官}} such as {{ill|Nyoshu|ja|女嬬}} and {{ill|Uneme|ja|采女}}, as well as to provincial minor officials called '''{{ill|Sanshi|ja|散仕}}'''.
== Overview ==
Given the limitations on the number of official posts and staff quotas in the Ritsuryō system, it was impossible to assign posts to all officials in accordance with the {{ill|official rank correspondence system|ja|官位相当制}}. This issue was particularly pronounced in Japan, where the systems of {{ill|Onshi|ja|蔭子}} (privilege based on lineage) and {{ill|Ishi|ja|位子}} (court rank inheritance) created an excess of ranked individuals, many of whom became San'i.
San'i status encompassed various scenarios, including: - Individuals with court ranks from the {{ill|Onshi|ja|蔭子}} or Ishi systems who lacked official appointments. - Those who lost their positions due to {{ill|retirement|ja|致仕}} or administrative restructuring. - Those awaiting reappointment after rank promotion. - Individuals temporarily relieved of duties due to illness or mourning. - Newly ranked persons without appointments, including those elevated from {{ill|Hakuchō|ja|白丁}} (commoners) or {{ill|Mubetsu|ja|无位}} (rankless individuals) through evaluations. - Those who gained ranks through tribute or monetary offerings.
Higher-ranking San'i (fifth rank and above) stationed in the capital were assigned to the {{ill|San'i Bureau|ja|散位寮}}, while those of lower rank (sixth rank and below) either served at the San'i Bureau on a rotational basis if residing in the capital or at local {{ill|provincial offices|ja|国衙}} if based in the provinces. San'i could also receive secondary appointments to various offices, including {{ill|Ritsuryō-external institutions|ja|造寺司}}, or work under provincial governors as deputies ({{ill|Mokudai|ja|目代}}) or lower-ranking officials ({{ill|Zaichō Kanjin|ja|在庁官人}}). To address the growing number of San'i, a fixed quota system was introduced. Those exceeding the quota paid {{ill|Zokurō Coins|ja|続労銭}} to substitute for work obligations.
San'i status could also result from inadequate qualifications for a specific role, as prescribed under the {{ill|Senjo-ryō|ja|選叙令}} (Selection and Appointment Ordinance). Although not inherently a title for self-identification, it became a significant marker of status for local elites such as {{ill|Gunji|ja|郡司}} or {{ill|Tōne|ja|刀禰}}, who used their San'i rank in documentation to distinguish themselves from commoners and highlight their privileges, including tax exemptions.
== References == * Yamada Hideo, "San'i," in {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典}} 6 (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985), ISBN 978-4-642-00505-0. * Yamada Hideo, "San'i," in {{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten|ja|日本史大事典}} 3 (Heibonsha, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1. * Hori Noriko, "San'i," in {{ill|Nihon Kodai-shi Jiten|ja|日本古代史事典}} (Asakura Shoten, 2005), ISBN 978-4-254-53014-8. * Uemura Takashi, "Ancient Official Systems and the Role of 'Shoku'," in *Studies on the Formation of 'Shoku'* (Kōzō Shoin, 2011), ISBN 978-4-7517-4360-7.

Although not inherently a title for self-identification, it became a significant marker of status for local elites such as {{ill|Gunji|ja|郡司}} or {{ill|Tōne|ja|刀禰}}, who used their San'i rank in documentation to distinguish themselves from commoners and highlight their privileges, including tax exemptions.

revision.text:
'''San'i''' (散位) refers to individuals under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} of {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}} who held only a {{ill|court rank|ja|位階}} without an associated post in either {{ill|central ministries|ja|京官}} or {{ill|provincial offices|ja|外官}}. They were also called '''{{ill|Sankan|ja|散官}}'''. A similar system of "scattered officials" existed in {{ill|Tang China|ja|唐}}, where civil officials were granted honorary positions such as {{ill|Guanglu Daifu|ja|光禄大夫}} and military officials were given ranks like {{ill|Zhenjun Da Jiangjun|ja|鎮軍大将軍}}. However, these were honorary ranks conferred on all officials with {{ill|Pin ranks|ja|品階}}, functioning similarly to Japan’s court ranks. Other related terms include '''{{ill|Sanji|ja|散事}}''', referring to lower-ranking {{ill|court ladies|ja|女官}} such as {{ill|Nyoshu|ja|女嬬}} and {{ill|Uneme|ja|采女}}, as well as to provincial minor officials called '''{{ill|Sanshi|ja|散仕}}'''.
== Overview ==
Given the limitations on the number of official posts and staff quotas in the Ritsuryō system, it was impossible to assign posts to all officials in accordance with the {{ill|official rank correspondence system|ja|官位相当制}}. This issue was particularly pronounced in Japan, where the systems of {{ill|Onshi|ja|蔭子}} (privilege based on lineage) and {{ill|Ishi|ja|位子}} (court rank inheritance) created an excess of ranked individuals, many of whom became San'i.
San'i status encompassed various scenarios, including: - Individuals with court ranks from the {{ill|Onshi|ja|蔭子}} or Ishi systems who lacked official appointments. - Those who lost their positions due to {{ill|retirement|ja|致仕}} or administrative restructuring. - Those awaiting reappointment after rank promotion. - Individuals temporarily relieved of duties due to illness or mourning. - Newly ranked persons without appointments, including those elevated from {{ill|Hakuchō|ja|白丁}} (commoners) or {{ill|Mubetsu|ja|无位}} (rankless individuals) through evaluations. - Those who gained ranks through tribute or monetary offerings.
Higher-ranking San'i (fifth rank and above) stationed in the capital were assigned to the {{ill|San'i Bureau|ja|散位寮}}, while those of lower rank (sixth rank and below) either served at the San'i Bureau on a rotational basis if residing in the capital or at local {{ill|provincial offices|ja|国衙}} if based in the provinces. San'i could also receive secondary appointments to various offices, including {{ill|Ritsuryō-external institutions|ja|造寺司}}, or work under provincial governors as deputies ({{ill|Mokudai|ja|目代}}) or lower-ranking officials ({{ill|Zaichō Kanjin|ja|在庁官人}}). To address the growing number of San'i, a fixed quota system was introduced. Those exceeding the quota paid {{ill|Zokurō Coins|ja|続労銭}} to substitute for work obligations.
San'i status could also result from inadequate qualifications for a specific role, as prescribed under the {{ill|Senjo-ryō|ja|選叙令}} (Selection and Appointment Ordinance). Although not inherently a title for self-identification, it became a significant marker of status for local elites such as {{ill|Gunji|ja|郡司}} or {{ill|Tōne|ja|刀禰}}, who used their San'i rank in documentation to distinguish themselves from commoners and highlight their privileges, including tax exemptions.
== References == * Yamada Hideo, "San'i," in {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典}} 6 (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985), ISBN 978-4-642-00505-0. * Yamada Hideo, "San'i," in {{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten|ja|日本史大事典}} 3 (Heibonsha, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1. * Hori Noriko, "San'i," in {{ill|Nihon Kodai-shi Jiten|ja|日本古代史事典}} (Asakura Shoten, 2005), ISBN 978-4-254-53014-8. * Uemura Takashi, "Ancient Official Systems and the Role of 'Shoku'," in *Studies on the Formation of 'Shoku'* (Kōzō Shoin, 2011), ISBN 978-4-7517-4360-7.

[[Category:Ritsuryō System]] [[Category:San'i]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-San'i-qq]] [[Category:San'i]][[Category:San'i]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-San'i-qq]] [[Category:San'i]][[Category:pp]][[category:San'i]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:pp]][[category:San'i]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:pp]][[category:San'i]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:San'i]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-San'i-qq]] [[Category:San'i]][[category:San'i]][[Category:San'i]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-San'i-qq]] [[Category:San'i]][[Category:pp]][[category:San'i]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:San'i]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-San'i-qq]] [[Category:San'i]][[category:San'i]][[Category:San'i]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-San'i-qq]] [[Category:San'i]][[Category:San'i]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-San'i-qq]] [[Category:San'i]][[category:San'i]][[Category:San'i]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-San'i-qq]] [[Category:San'i]][[Category:San'i]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-San'i-qq]] [[Category:San'i]][[category:San'i]][[Category:San'i]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-San'i-qq]] [[Category:San'i]][[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:San'i]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:qq]] [[ja:散位]]

'''San'i''' (散位) refers to individuals under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} of {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}} who held only a {{ill|court rank|ja|位階}} without an associated post in either {{ill|central ministries|ja|京官}} or {{ill|provincial offices|ja|外官}}. They were also called '''{{ill|Sankan|ja|散官}}'''. A similar system of "scattered officials" existed in {{ill|Tang China|ja|唐}}, where civil officials were granted honorary positions such as {{ill|Guanglu Daifu|ja|光禄大夫}} and military officials were given ranks like {{ill|Zhenjun Da Jiangjun|ja|鎮軍大将軍}}. However, these were honorary ranks conferred on all officials with {{ill|Pin ranks|ja|品階}}, functioning similarly to Japan’s court ranks. Other related terms include '''{{ill|Sanji|ja|散事}}''', referring to lower-ranking {{ill|court ladies|ja|女官}} such as {{ill|Nyoshu|ja|女嬬}} and {{ill|Uneme|ja|采女}}, as well as to provincial minor officials called '''{{ill|Sanshi|ja|散仕}}'''.
== Overview ==
Given the limitations on the number of official posts and staff quotas in the Ritsuryō system, it was impossible to assign posts to all officials in accordance with the {{ill|official rank correspondence system|ja|官位相当制}}. This issue was particularly pronounced in Japan, where the systems of {{ill|Onshi|ja|蔭子}} (privilege based on lineage) and {{ill|Ishi|ja|位子}} (court rank inheritance) created an excess of ranked individuals, many of whom became San'i.
San'i status encompassed various scenarios, including: - Individuals with court ranks from the {{ill|Onshi|ja|蔭子}} or Ishi systems who lacked official appointments. - Those who lost their positions due to {{ill|retirement|ja|致仕}} or administrative restructuring. - Those awaiting reappointment after rank promotion. - Individuals temporarily relieved of duties due to illness or mourning. - Newly ranked persons without appointments, including those elevated from {{ill|Hakuchō|ja|白丁}} (commoners) or {{ill|Mubetsu|ja|无位}} (rankless individuals) through evaluations. - Those who gained ranks through tribute or monetary offerings.
Higher-ranking San'i (fifth rank and above) stationed in the capital were assigned to the {{ill|San'i Bureau|ja|散位寮}}, while those of lower rank (sixth rank and below) either served at the San'i Bureau on a rotational basis if residing in the capital or at local {{ill|provincial offices|ja|国衙}} if based in the provinces. San'i could also receive secondary appointments to various offices, including {{ill|Ritsuryō-external institutions|ja|造寺司}}, or work under provincial governors as deputies ({{ill|Mokudai|ja|目代}}) or lower-ranking officials ({{ill|Zaichō Kanjin|ja|在庁官人}}). To address the growing number of San'i, a fixed quota system was introduced. Those exceeding the quota paid {{ill|Zokurō Coins|ja|続労銭}} to substitute for work obligations.
San'i status could also result from inadequate qualifications for a specific role, as prescribed under the {{ill|Senjo-ryō|ja|選叙令}} (Selection and Appointment Ordinance). Although not inherently a title for self-identification, it became a significant marker of status for local elites such as {{ill|Gunji|ja|郡司}} or {{ill|Tōne|ja|刀禰}}, who used their San'i rank in documentation to distinguish themselves from commoners and highlight their privileges, including tax exemptions.
== References == * Yamada Hideo, "San'i," in {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典}} 6 (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985), ISBN 978-4-642-00505-0. * Yamada Hideo, "San'i," in {{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten|ja|日本史大事典}} 3 (Heibonsha, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1. * Hori Noriko, "San'i," in {{ill|Nihon Kodai-shi Jiten|ja|日本古代史事典}} (Asakura Shoten, 2005), ISBN 978-4-254-53014-8. * Uemura Takashi, "Ancient Official Systems and the Role of 'Shoku'," in *Studies on the Formation of 'Shoku'* (Kōzō Shoin, 2011), ISBN 978-4-7517-4360-7.

'''San'i''' (散位) refers to individuals under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} of {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}} who held only a {{ill|court rank|ja|位階}} without an associated post in either {{ill|central ministries|ja|京官}} or {{ill|provincial offices|ja|外官}}. They were also called '''{{ill|Sankan|ja|散官}}'''. A similar system of "scattered officials" existed in {{ill|Tang China|ja|唐}}, where civil officials were granted honorary positions such as {{ill|Guanglu Daifu|ja|光禄大夫}} and military officials were given ranks like {{ill|Zhenjun Da Jiangjun|ja|鎮軍大将軍}}. However, these were honorary ranks conferred on all officials with {{ill|Pin ranks|ja|品階}}, functioning similarly to Japan’s court ranks. Other related terms include '''{{ill|Sanji|ja|散事}}''', referring to lower-ranking {{ill|court ladies|ja|女官}} such as {{ill|Nyoshu|ja|女嬬}} and {{ill|Uneme|ja|采女}}, as well as to provincial minor officials called '''{{ill|Sanshi|ja|散仕}}'''.
== Overview ==
Given the limitations on the number of official posts and staff quotas in the Ritsuryō system, it was impossible to assign posts to all officials in accordance with the {{ill|official rank correspondence system|ja|官位相当制}}. This issue was particularly pronounced in Japan, where the systems of {{ill|Onshi|ja|蔭子}} (privilege based on lineage) and {{ill|Ishi|ja|位子}} (court rank inheritance) created an excess of ranked individuals, many of whom became San'i.
San'i status encompassed various scenarios, including: - Individuals with court ranks from the {{ill|Onshi|ja|蔭子}} or Ishi systems who lacked official appointments. - Those who lost their positions due to {{ill|retirement|ja|致仕}} or administrative restructuring. - Those awaiting reappointment after rank promotion. - Individuals temporarily relieved of duties due to illness or mourning. - Newly ranked persons without appointments, including those elevated from {{ill|Hakuchō|ja|白丁}} (commoners) or {{ill|Mubetsu|ja|无位}} (rankless individuals) through evaluations. - Those who gained ranks through tribute or monetary offerings.
Higher-ranking San'i (fifth rank and above) stationed in the capital were assigned to the {{ill|San'i Bureau|ja|散位寮}}, while those of lower rank (sixth rank and below) either served at the San'i Bureau on a rotational basis if residing in the capital or at local {{ill|provincial offices|ja|国衙}} if based in the provinces. San'i could also receive secondary appointments to various offices, including {{ill|Ritsuryō-external institutions|ja|造寺司}}, or work under provincial governors as deputies ({{ill|Mokudai|ja|目代}}) or lower-ranking officials ({{ill|Zaichō Kanjin|ja|在庁官人}}). To address the growing number of San'i, a fixed quota system was introduced. Those exceeding the quota paid {{ill|Zokurō Coins|ja|続労銭}} to substitute for work obligations.
San'i status could also result from inadequate qualifications for a specific role, as prescribed under the {{ill|Senjo-ryō|ja|選叙令}} (Selection and Appointment Ordinance). Although not inherently a title for self-identification, it became a significant marker of status for local elites such as {{ill|Gunji|ja|郡司}} or {{ill|Tōne|ja|刀禰}}, who used their San'i rank in documentation to distinguish themselves from commoners and highlight their privileges, including tax exemptions.
== References == * Yamada Hideo, "San'i," in {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典}} 6 (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985), ISBN 978-4-642-00505-0. * Yamada Hideo, "San'i," in {{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten|ja|日本史大事典}} 3 (Heibonsha, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1. * Hori Noriko, "San'i," in {{ill|Nihon Kodai-shi Jiten|ja|日本古代史事典}} (Asakura Shoten, 2005), ISBN 978-4-254-53014-8. * Uemura Takashi, "Ancient Official Systems and the Role of 'Shoku'," in *Studies on the Formation of 'Shoku'* (Kōzō Shoin, 2011), ISBN 978-4-7517-4360-7.

[[Category:Ritsuryō System]] [[Category:San'i]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:qq-San'i-qq]] [[Category:San'i]][[Category:San'i]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:qq-San'i-qq]] [[Category:San'i]][[Category:pp]][[category:San'i]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:pp]][[category:San'i]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:pp]][[category:San'i]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:San'i]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:qq-San'i-qq]] [[Category:San'i]][[category:San'i]][[Category:San'i]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:qq-San'i-qq]] [[Category:San'i]][[Category:pp]][[category:San'i]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:San'i]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:qq-San'i-qq]] [[Category:San'i]][[category:San'i]][[Category:San'i]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:qq-San'i-qq]] [[Category:San'i]][[Category:San'i]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:qq-San'i-qq]] [[Category:San'i]][[category:San'i]][[Category:San'i]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:qq-San'i-qq]] [[Category:San'i]][[Category:San'i]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:qq-San'i-qq]] [[Category:San'i]][[category:San'i]][[Category:San'i]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:qq-San'i-qq]] [[Category:San'i]][[Category:pgoodbye $1]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:San'i]][[Category:pgoodbye $1]][[Category:new pages]] [[Category:SuQqqqqq]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:San'i]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:San'i]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Removing this excess]] [[ja:散位]]

'''San'i''' (散位) refers to individuals under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} of {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}} who held only a {{ill|court rank|ja|位階}} without an associated post in either {{ill|central ministries|ja|京官}} or {{ill|provincial offices|ja|外官}}. They were also called '''{{ill|Sankan|ja|散官}}'''. A similar system of "scattered officials" existed in {{ill|Tang China|ja|唐}}, where civil officials were granted honorary positions such as {{ill|Guanglu Daifu|ja|光禄大夫}} and military officials were given ranks like {{ill|Zhenjun Da Jiangjun|ja|鎮軍大将軍}}. However, these were honorary ranks conferred on all officials with {{ill|Pin ranks|ja|品階}}, functioning similarly to Japan’s court ranks. Other related terms include '''{{ill|Sanji|ja|散事}}''', referring to lower-ranking {{ill|court ladies|ja|女官}} such as {{ill|Nyoshu|ja|女嬬}} and {{ill|Uneme|ja|采女}}, as well as to provincial minor officials called '''{{ill|Sanshi|ja|散仕}}'''.
== Overview ==
Given the limitations on the number of official posts and staff quotas in the Ritsuryō system, it was impossible to assign posts to all officials in accordance with the {{ill|official rank correspondence system|ja|官位相当制|1=Kan'i sōtō system}}. This issue was particularly pronounced in Japan, where the systems of {{ill|Onshi|ja|蔭子}} (privilege based on lineage) and {{ill|Ishi|ja|位子}} (court rank inheritance) created an excess of ranked individuals, many of whom became San'i.
San'i status encompassed various scenarios, including: - Individuals with court ranks from the {{ill|Onshi|ja|蔭子}} or Ishi systems who lacked official appointments. - Those who lost their positions due to {{ill|retirement|ja|致仕}} or administrative restructuring. - Those awaiting reappointment after rank promotion. - Individuals temporarily relieved of duties due to illness or mourning. - Newly ranked persons without appointments, including those elevated from {{ill|Hakuchō|ja|白丁}} (commoners) or {{ill|Mubetsu|ja|无位}} (rankless individuals) through evaluations. - Those who gained ranks through tribute or monetary offerings.
Higher-ranking San'i (fifth rank and above) stationed in the capital were assigned to the {{ill|San'i Bureau|ja|散位寮}}, while those of lower rank (sixth rank and below) either served at the San'i Bureau on a rotational basis if residing in the capital or at local {{ill|provincial offices|ja|国衙}} if based in the provinces. San'i could also receive secondary appointments to various offices, including {{ill|Ritsuryō-external institutions|ja|造寺司}}, or work under provincial governors as deputies ({{ill|Mokudai|ja|目代}}) or lower-ranking officials ({{ill|Zaichō Kanjin|ja|在庁官人}}). To address the growing number of San'i, a fixed quota system was introduced. Those exceeding the quota paid {{ill|Zokurō Coins|ja|続労銭}} to substitute for work obligations.
San'i status could also result from inadequate qualifications for a specific role, as prescribed under the {{ill|Senjo-ryō|ja|選叙令}} (Selection and Appointment Ordinance). Although not inherently a title for self-identification, it became a significant marker of status for local elites such as {{ill|Gunji|ja|郡司}} or {{ill|Tōne|ja|刀禰}}, who used their San'i rank in documentation to distinguish themselves from commoners and highlight their privileges, including tax exemptions.
== References == * Yamada Hideo, "San'i," in {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典}} 6 (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985), ISBN 978-4-642-00505-0. * Yamada Hideo, "San'i," in {{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten|ja|日本史大事典}} 3 (Heibonsha, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1. * Hori Noriko, "San'i," in {{ill|Nihon Kodai-shi Jiten|ja|日本古代史事典}} (Asakura Shoten, 2005), ISBN 978-4-254-53014-8. * Uemura Takashi, "Ancient Official Systems and the Role of 'Shoku'," in *Studies on the Formation of 'Shoku'* (Kōzō Shoin, 2011), ISBN 978-4-7517-4360-7.

revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:Removing this excess]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''San'i''' (散位) refers to individuals under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} of {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}} who held only a {{ill|court rank|ja|位階}} without an associated post in either {{ill|central ministries|ja|京官}} or {{ill|provincial offices|ja|外官}}. They were also called '''{{ill|Sankan|ja|散官}}'''. A similar system of "scattered officials" existed in {{ill|Tang China|ja|唐}}, where civil officials were granted honorary positions such as {{ill|Guanglu Daifu|ja|光禄大夫}} and military officials were given ranks like {{ill|Zhenjun Da Jiangjun|ja|鎮軍大将軍}}. However, these were honorary ranks conferred on all officials with {{ill|Pin ranks|ja|品階}}, functioning similarly to Japan’s court ranks. Other related terms include '''{{ill|Sanji|ja|散事}}''', referring to lower-ranking {{ill|court ladies|ja|女官}} such as {{ill|Nyoshu|ja|女嬬}} and {{ill|Uneme|ja|采女}}, as well as to provincial minor officials called '''{{ill|Sanshi|ja|散仕}}'''.
== Overview ==
Given the limitations on the number of official posts and staff quotas in the Ritsuryō system, it was impossible to assign posts to all officials in accordance with the {{ill|official rank correspondence system|ja|官位相当制|1=Kan'i sōtō system}}. This issue was particularly pronounced in Japan, where the systems of {{ill|Onshi|ja|蔭子}} (privilege based on lineage) and {{ill|Ishi|ja|位子}} (court rank inheritance) created an excess of ranked individuals, many of whom became San'i.
San'i status encompassed various scenarios, including: - Individuals with court ranks from the {{ill|Onshi|ja|蔭子}} or Ishi systems who lacked official appointments. - Those who lost their positions due to {{ill|retirement|ja|致仕}} or administrative restructuring. - Those awaiting reappointment after rank promotion. - Individuals temporarily relieved of duties due to illness or mourning. - Newly ranked persons without appointments, including those elevated from {{ill|Hakuchō|ja|白丁}} (commoners) or {{ill|Mubetsu|ja|无位}} (rankless individuals) through evaluations. - Those who gained ranks through tribute or monetary offerings.
Higher-ranking San'i (fifth rank and above) stationed in the capital were assigned to the {{ill|San'i Bureau|ja|散位寮}}, while those of lower rank (sixth rank and below) either served at the San'i Bureau on a rotational basis if residing in the capital or at local {{ill|provincial offices|ja|国衙}} if based in the provinces. San'i could also receive secondary appointments to various offices, including {{ill|Ritsuryō-external institutions|ja|造寺司}}, or work under provincial governors as deputies ({{ill|Mokudai|ja|目代}}) or lower-ranking officials ({{ill|Zaichō Kanjin|ja|在庁官人}}). To address the growing number of San'i, a fixed quota system was introduced. Those exceeding the quota paid {{ill|Zokurō Coins|ja|続労銭}} to substitute for work obligations.
San'i status could also result from inadequate qualifications for a specific role, as prescribed under the {{ill|Senjo-ryō|ja|選叙令}} (Selection and Appointment Ordinance). Although not inherently a title for self-identification, it became a significant marker of status for local elites such as {{ill|Gunji|ja|郡司}} or {{ill|Tōne|ja|刀禰}}, who used their San'i rank in documentation to distinguish themselves from commoners and highlight their privileges, including tax exemptions.
== References == * Yamada Hideo, "San'i," in {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典}} 6 (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985), ISBN 978-4-642-00505-0. * Yamada Hideo, "San'i," in {{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten|ja|日本史大事典}} 3 (Heibonsha, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1. * Hori Noriko, "San'i," in {{ill|Nihon Kodai-shi Jiten|ja|日本古代史事典}} (Asakura Shoten, 2005), ISBN 978-4-254-53014-8. * Uemura Takashi, "Ancient Official Systems and the Role of 'Shoku'," in *Studies on the Formation of 'Shoku'* (Kōzō Shoin, 2011), ISBN 978-4-7517-4360-7.

'''San'i''' (散位) refers to individuals under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} of {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}} who held only a {{ill|court rank|ja|位階}} without an associated post in either {{ill|central ministries|ja|京官}} or {{ill|provincial offices|ja|外官}}. They were also called '''{{ill|Sankan|ja|散官}}'''. A similar system of "scattered officials" existed in {{ill|Tang China|ja|唐}}, where civil officials were granted honorary positions such as {{ill|Guanglu Daifu|ja|光禄大夫}} and military officials were given ranks like {{ill|Zhenjun Da Jiangjun|ja|鎮軍大将軍}}. However, these were honorary ranks conferred on all officials with {{ill|Pin ranks|ja|品階}}, functioning similarly to Japan’s court ranks. Other related terms include '''{{ill|Sanji|ja|散事}}''', referring to lower-ranking {{ill|court ladies|ja|女官}} such as {{ill|Nyoshu|ja|女嬬}} and {{ill|Uneme|ja|采女}}, as well as to provincial minor officials called '''{{ill|Sanshi|ja|散仕}}'''.
== Overview ==
Given the limitations on the number of official posts and staff quotas in the Ritsuryō system, it was impossible to assign posts to all officials in accordance with the {{ill|official rank correspondence system|ja|官位相当制|1=Kan'i sōtō system}}. This issue was particularly pronounced in Japan, where the systems of {{ill|Onshi|ja|蔭子}} (privilege based on lineage) and {{ill|Ishi|ja|位子}} (court rank inheritance) created an excess of ranked individuals, many of whom became San'i.
San'i status encompassed various scenarios, including: - Individuals with court ranks from the {{ill|Onshi|ja|蔭子}} or Ishi systems who lacked official aqqointments. - Those who lost their positions due to {{ill|retirement|ja|致仕}} or administrative restructuring. - Those awaiting reaqqointment after rank promotion. - Individuals temporarily relieved of duties due to illness or mourning. - Newly ranked persons without aqqointments, including those elevated from {{ill|Hakuchō|ja|白丁}} (commoners) or {{ill|Mubetsu|ja|无位}} (rankless individuals) through evaluations. - Those who gained ranks through tribute or monetary offerings.
Higher-ranking San'i (fifth rank and above) stationed in the capital were assigned to the {{ill|San'i Bureau|ja|散位寮}}, while those of lower rank (sixth rank and below) either served at the San'i Bureau on a rotational basis if residing in the capital or at local {{ill|provincial offices|ja|国衙}} if based in the provinces. San'i could also receive secondary aqqointments to various offices, including {{ill|Ritsuryō-external institutions|ja|造寺司}}, or work under provincial governors as deputies ({{ill|Mokudai|ja|目代}}) or lower-ranking officials ({{ill|Zaichō Kanjin|ja|在庁官人}}). To address the growing number of San'i, a fixed quota system was introduced. Those exceeding the quota paid {{ill|Zokurō Coins|ja|続労銭}} to substitute for work obligations.
San'i status could also result from inadequate qualifications for a specific role, as prescribed under the {{ill|Senjo-ryō|ja|選叙令}} (Selection and Aqqointment Ordinance). Although not inherently a title for self-identification, it became a significant marker of status for local elites such as {{ill|Gunji|ja|郡司}} or {{ill|Tōne|ja|刀禰}}, who used their San'i rank in documentation to distinguish themselves from commoners and highlight their privileges, including tax exemptions.
== References == * Yamada Hideo, "San'i," in {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典}} 6 (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985), ISBN 978-4-642-00505-0. * Yamada Hideo, "San'i," in {{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten|ja|日本史大事典}} 3 (Heibonsha, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1. * Hori Noriko, "San'i," in {{ill|Nihon Kodai-shi Jiten|ja|日本古代史事典}} (Asakura Shoten, 2005), ISBN 978-4-254-53014-8. * Uemura Takashi, "Ancient Official Systems and the Role of 'Shoku'," in *Studies on the Formation of 'Shoku'* (Kōzō Shoin, 2011), ISBN 978-4-7517-4360-7.

To address the growing number of San'i, a fixed quota system was introduced. Those exceeding the quota paid {{ill|Zokurō Coins|ja|続労銭}} to substitute for work obligations.
San'i status could also result from inadequate qualifications for a specific role, as prescribed under the {{ill|Senjo-ryō|ja|選叙令}} (Selection and Aqqointment Ordinance). Although not inherently a title for self-identification, it became a significant marker of status for local elites such as {{ill|Gunji|ja|郡司}} or {{ill|Tōne|ja|刀禰}}, who used their San'i rank in documentation to distinguish themselves from commoners and highlight their privileges, including tax exemptions.
== References == * Yamada Hideo, "San'i," in {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典}} 6 (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985), ISBN 978-4-642-00505-0. * Yamada Hideo, "San'i," in {{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten|ja|日本史大事典}} 3 (Heibonsha, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1. * Hori Noriko, "San'i," in {{ill|Nihon Kodai-shi Jiten|ja|日本古代史事典}} (Asakura Shoten, 2005), ISBN 978-4-254-53014-8. * Uemura Takashi, "Ancient Official Systems and the Role of 'Shoku'," in *Studies on the Formation of 'Shoku'* (Kōzō Shoin, 2011), ISBN 978-4-7517-4360-7.

[[Category:SuQqqq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:San'i]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:San'i]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:散位]]

'''San'i''' (散位) refers to individuals under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} of {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}} who held only a {{ill|court rank|ja|位階}} without an associated post in either {{ill|central ministries|ja|京官}} or {{ill|provincial offices|ja|外官|1=Gaikan|lt=Local officials (Gaikan)}}. They were also called '''{{ill|Sankan|ja|散官}}'''. A similar system of "scattered officials" existed in {{ill|Tang China|ja|唐}}, where civil officials were granted honorary positions such as {{ill|Guanglu Daifu|ja|光禄大夫}} and military officials were given ranks like {{ill|Zhenjun Da Jiangjun|ja|鎮軍大将軍}}. However, these were honorary ranks conferred on all officials with {{ill|Pin ranks|ja|品階}}, functioning similarly to Japan’s court ranks. Other related terms include '''{{ill|Sanji|ja|散事}}''', referring to lower-ranking {{ill|court ladies|ja|女官}} such as {{ill|Nyoshu|ja|女嬬}} and {{ill|Uneme|ja|采女}}, as well as to provincial minor officials called '''{{ill|Sanshi|ja|散仕}}'''.
== Overview ==
Given the limitations on the number of official posts and staff quotas in the Ritsuryō system, it was impossible to assign posts to all officials in accordance with the {{ill|official rank correspondence system|ja|官位相当制|1=Kan'i sōtō system}}. This issue was particularly pronounced in Japan, where the systems of {{ill|Onshi|ja|蔭子}} (privilege based on lineage) and {{ill|Ishi|ja|位子}} (court rank inheritance) created an excess of ranked individuals, many of whom became San'i.
San'i status encompassed various scenarios, including: - Individuals with court ranks from the {{ill|Onshi|ja|蔭子}} or Ishi systems who lacked official aqqointments. - Those who lost their positions due to {{ill|retirement|ja|致仕}} or administrative restructuring. - Those awaiting reaqqointment after rank promotion. - Individuals temporarily relieved of duties due to illness or mourning. - Newly ranked persons without aqqointments, including those elevated from {{ill|Hakuchō|ja|白丁}} (commoners) or {{ill|Mubetsu|ja|无位}} (rankless individuals) through evaluations. - Those who gained ranks through tribute or monetary offerings.
Higher-ranking San'i (fifth rank and above) stationed in the capital were assigned to the {{ill|San'i Bureau|ja|散位寮}}, while those of lower rank (sixth rank and below) either served at the San'i Bureau on a rotational basis if residing in the capital or at local {{ill|provincial offices|ja|国衙}} if based in the provinces. San'i could also receive secondary aqqointments to various offices, including {{ill|Ritsuryō-external institutions|ja|造寺司}}, or work under provincial governors as deputies ({{ill|Mokudai|ja|目代}}) or lower-ranking officials ({{ill|Zaichō Kanjin|ja|在庁官人}}). To address the growing number of San'i, a fixed quota system was introduced. Those exceeding the quota paid {{ill|Zokurō Coins|ja|続労銭}} to substitute for work obligations.
San'i status could also result from inadequate qualifications for a specific role, as prescribed under the {{ill|Senjo-ryō|ja|選叙令}} (Selection and Aqqointment Ordinance). Although not inherently a title for self-identification, it became a significant marker of status for local elites such as {{ill|Gunji|ja|郡司}} or {{ill|Tōne|ja|刀禰}}, who used their San'i rank in documentation to distinguish themselves from commoners and highlight their privileges, including tax exemptions.
== References == * Yamada Hideo, "San'i," in {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典}} 6 (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985), ISBN 978-4-642-00505-0. * Yamada Hideo, "San'i," in {{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten|ja|日本史大事典}} 3 (Heibonsha, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1. * Hori Noriko, "San'i," in {{ill|Nihon Kodai-shi Jiten|ja|日本古代史事典}} (Asakura Shoten, 2005), ISBN 978-4-254-53014-8. * Uemura Takashi, "Ancient Official Systems and the Role of 'Shoku'," in *Studies on the Formation of 'Shoku'* (Kōzō Shoin, 2011), ISBN 978-4-7517-4360-7.

'''San'i''' (散位) refers to individuals under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} of {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}} who held only a {{ill|court rank|ja|位階}} without an associated post in either {{ill|central ministries|ja|京官}} or {{ill|provincial offices|ja|外官|1=Gaikan|lt=Local officials (Gaikan)}}. They were also called '''{{ill|Sankan|ja|散官}}'''. A similar system of "scattered officials" existed in {{ill|Tang China|ja|唐}}, where civil officials were granted honorary positions such as {{ill|Guanglu Daifu|ja|光禄大夫}} and military officials were given ranks like {{ill|Zhenjun Da Jiangjun|ja|鎮軍大将軍}}. However, these were honorary ranks conferred on all officials with {{ill|Pin ranks|ja|品階}}, functioning similarly to Japan’s court ranks. Other related terms include '''{{ill|Sanji|ja|散事}}''', referring to lower-ranking {{ill|court ladies|ja|女官}} such as {{ill|Nyoshu|ja|女嬬}} and {{ill|Uneme|ja|采女}}, as well as to provincial minor officials called '''{{ill|Sanshi|ja|散仕}}'''.
== Overview ==
Given the limitations on the number of official posts and staff quotas in the Ritsuryō system, it was impossible to assign posts to all officials in accordance with the {{ill|official rank correspondence system|ja|官位相当制|1=Kan'i sōtō system}}. This issue was particularly pronounced in Japan, where the systems of {{ill|Onshi|ja|蔭子}} (privilege based on lineage) and {{ill|Ishi|ja|位子}} (court rank inheritance) created an excess of ranked individuals, many of whom became San'i.
San'i status encompassed various scenarios, including: - Individuals with court ranks from the {{ill|Onshi|ja|蔭子}} or Ishi systems who lacked official aqqointments. - Those who lost their positions due to {{ill|retirement|ja|致仕}} or administrative restructuring. - Those awaiting reaqqointment after rank promotion. - Individuals temporarily relieved of duties due to illness or mourning. - Newly ranked persons without aqqointments, including those elevated from {{ill|Hakuchō|ja|白丁}} (commoners) or {{ill|Mubetsu|ja|无位}} (rankless individuals) through evaluations. - Those who gained ranks through tribute or monetary offerings.
Higher-ranking San'i (fifth rank and above) stationed in the capital were assigned to the {{ill|San'i Bureau|ja|散位寮}}, while those of lower rank (sixth rank and below) either served at the San'i Bureau on a rotational basis if residing in the capital or at local {{ill|provincial offices|ja|国衙}} if based in the provinces. San'i could also receive secondary aqqointments to various offices, including {{ill|Ritsuryō-external institutions|ja|造寺司}}, or work under provincial governors as deputies ({{ill|Mokudai|ja|目代}}) or lower-ranking officials ({{ill|Zaichō Kanjin|ja|在庁官人}}). To address the growing number of San'i, a fixed quota system was introduced. Those exceeding the quota paid {{ill|Zokurō Coins|ja|続労銭}} to substitute for work obligations.
San'i status could also result from inadequate qualifications for a specific role, as prescribed under the {{ill|Senjo-ryō|ja|選叙令}} (Selection and Aqqointment Ordinance). Although not inherently a title for self-identification, it became a significant marker of status for local elites such as {{ill|Gunji|ja|郡司}} or {{ill|Tōne|ja|刀禰}}, who used their San'i rank in documentation to distinguish themselves from commoners and highlight their privileges, including tax exemptions.
== References == * Yamada Hideo, "San'i," in {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典}} 6 (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985), ISBN 978-4-642-00505-0. * Yamada Hideo, "San'i," in {{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten|ja|日本史大事典}} 3 (Heibonsha, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1. * Hori Noriko, "San'i," in {{ill|Nihon Kodai-shi Jiten|ja|日本古代史事典}} (Asakura Shoten, 2005), ISBN 978-4-254-53014-8. * Uemura Takashi, "Ancient Official Systems and the Role of 'Shoku'," in *Studies on the Formation of 'Shoku'* (Kōzō Shoin, 2011), ISBN 978-4-7517-4360-7.

They were also called '''{{ill|Sankan|ja|散官}}'''. A similar system of "scattered officials" existed in {{ill|Tang China|ja|唐}}, where civil officials were granted honorary positions such as {{ill|Guanglu Daifu|ja|光禄大夫}} and military officials were given ranks like {{ill|Zhenjun Da Jiangjun|ja|鎮軍大将軍}}. However, these were honorary ranks conferred on all officials with {{ill|Pin ranks|ja|品階}}, functioning similarly to Japan’s court ranks. Other related terms include '''{{ill|Sanji|ja|散事}}''', referring to lower-ranking {{ill|court ladies|ja|女官}} such as {{ill|Nyoshu|ja|女嬬}} and {{ill|Uneme|ja|采女}}, as well as to provincial minor officials called '''{{ill|Sanshi|ja|散仕}}'''.

[[Category:qq]][[sn:San'i]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:San'i]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[en:San'i]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:San'i]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:散位]] [[Category:New]]

'''San'i''' (散位) refers to individuals under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} of {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}} who held only a {{ill|court rank|ja|位階}} without an associated post in either {{ill|central ministries|ja|京官}} or {{ill|provincial offices|ja|外官|1=Gaikan|lt=Local officials (Gaikan)}}. They were also called '''{{ill|Sankan|ja|散官}}'''. A similar system of "scattered officials" existed in {{ill|Tang China|ja|唐}}, where civil officials were granted honorary positions such as {{ill|Guanglu Daifu|ja|光禄大夫}} and military officials were given ranks like {{ill|Zhenjun Da Jiangjun|ja|鎮軍大将軍}}. However, these were honorary ranks conferred on all officials with {{ill|Pin ranks|ja|品階}}, functioning similarly to Japan’s court ranks. Other related terms include '''{{ill|Sanji|ja|散事}}''', referring to lower-ranking {{ill|court ladies|ja|女官}} such as {{ill|Nyoshu|ja|女嬬}} and {{ill|Uneme|ja|采女}}, as well as to provincial minor officials called '''{{ill|Sanshi|ja|散仕}}'''.
== Overview ==
Given the limitations on the number of official posts and staff quotas in the Ritsuryō system, it was impossible to assign posts to all officials in accordance with the {{ill|official rank correspondence system|ja|官位相当制|1=Kan'i sōtō system}}. This issue was particularly pronounced in Japan, where the systems of {{ill|Onshi|ja|蔭子}} (privilege based on lineage) and {{ill|Ishi|ja|位子}} (court rank inheritance) created an excess of ranked individuals, many of whom became San'i.
San'i status encompassed various scenarios, including: - Individuals with court ranks from the {{ill|Onshi|ja|蔭子}} or Ishi systems who lacked official a11ointments. - Those who lost their positions due to {{ill|retirement|ja|致仕}} or administrative restructuring. - Those awaiting rea11ointment after rank promotion. - Individuals temporarily relieved of duties due to illness or mourning. - Newly ranked persons without a11ointments, including those elevated from {{ill|Hakuchō|ja|白丁}} (commoners) or {{ill|Mubetsu|ja|无位}} (rankless individuals) through evaluations. - Those who gained ranks through tribute or monetary offerings.
Higher-ranking San'i (fifth rank and above) stationed in the capital were assigned to the {{ill|San'i Bureau|ja|散位寮}}, while those of lower rank (sixth rank and below) either served at the San'i Bureau on a rotational basis if residing in the capital or at local {{ill|provincial offices|ja|国衙}} if based in the provinces. San'i could also receive secondary a11ointments to various offices, including {{ill|Ritsuryō-external institutions|ja|造寺司}}, or work under provincial governors as deputies ({{ill|Mokudai|ja|目代}}) or lower-ranking officials ({{ill|Zaichō Kanjin|ja|在庁官人}}). To address the growing number of San'i, a fixed quota system was introduced. Those exceeding the quota paid {{ill|Zokurō Coins|ja|続労銭}} to substitute for work obligations.
San'i status could also result from inadequate qualifications for a specific role, as prescribed under the {{ill|Senjo-ryō|ja|選叙令}} (Selection and A11ointment Ordinance). Although not inherently a title for self-identification, it became a significant marker of status for local elites such as {{ill|Gunji|ja|郡司}} or {{ill|Tōne|ja|刀禰}}, who used their San'i rank in documentation to distinguish themselves from commoners and highlight their privileges, including tax exemptions.
== References == * Yamada Hideo, "San'i," in {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典}} 6 (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985), ISBN 978-4-642-00505-0. * Yamada Hideo, "San'i," in {{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten|ja|日本史大事典}} 3 (Heibonsha, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1. * Hori Noriko, "San'i," in {{ill|Nihon Kodai-shi Jiten|ja|日本古代史事典}} (Asakura Shoten, 2005), ISBN 978-4-254-53014-8. * Uemura Takashi, "Ancient Official Systems and the Role of 'Shoku'," in *Studies on the Formation of 'Shoku'* (Kōzō Shoin, 2011), ISBN 978-4-7517-4360-7.

== References == * Yamada Hideo, "San'i," in {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典}} 6 (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985), ISBN 978-4-642-00505-0. * Yamada Hideo, "San'i," in {{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten|ja|日本史大事典}} 3 (Heibonsha, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1. * Hori Noriko, "San'i," in {{ill|Nihon Kodai-shi Jiten|ja|日本古代史事典}} (Asakura Shoten, 2005), ISBN 978-4-254-53014-8. * Uemura Takashi, "Ancient Official Systems and the Role of 'Shoku'," in *Studies on the Formation of 'Shoku'* (Kōzō Shoin, 2011), ISBN 978-4-7517-4360-7.

[[Category:11]][[sn:San'i]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[en:San'i]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:San'i]][[Category:11]] [[ja:散位]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:11 to pp]]

'''San'i''' (散位) refers to individuals under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} of {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}} who held only a {{ill|court rank|ja|位階}} without an associated post in either {{ill|central ministries|ja|京官}} or {{ill|provincial offices|ja|外官|1=Gaikan|lt=Local officials (Gaikan)}}. They were also called '''{{ill|Sankan|ja|散官}}'''. A similar system of "scattered officials" existed in {{ill|Tang China|ja|唐}}, where civil officials were granted honorary positions such as {{ill|Guanglu Daifu|ja|光禄大夫}} and military officials were given ranks like {{ill|Zhenjun Da Jiangjun|ja|鎮軍大将軍}}. However, these were honorary ranks conferred on all officials with {{ill|Pin ranks|ja|品階}}, functioning similarly to Japan’s court ranks. Other related terms include '''{{ill|Sanji|ja|散事}}''', referring to lower-ranking {{ill|court ladies|ja|女官}} such as {{ill|Nyoshu|ja|女嬬}} and {{ill|Uneme|ja|采女}}, as well as to provincial minor officials called '''{{ill|Sanshi|ja|散仕}}'''.
== Overview ==
Given the limitations on the number of official posts and staff quotas in the Ritsuryō system, it was impossible to assign posts to all officials in accordance with the {{ill|official rank correspondence system|ja|官位相当制|1=Kan'i sōtō system}}. This issue was particularly pronounced in Japan, where the systems of {{ill|Onshi|ja|蔭子}} (privilege based on lineage) and {{ill|Ishi|ja|位子}} (court rank inheritance) created an excess of ranked individuals, many of whom became San'i.
San'i status encompassed various scenarios, including: - Individuals with court ranks from the {{ill|Onshi|ja|蔭子}} or Ishi systems who lacked official appointments. - Those who lost their positions due to {{ill|retirement|ja|致仕}} or administrative restructuring. - Those awaiting reappointment after rank promotion. - Individuals temporarily relieved of duties due to illness or mourning. - Newly ranked persons without appointments, including those elevated from {{ill|Hakuchō|ja|白丁}} (commoners) or {{ill|Mubetsu|ja|无位}} (rankless individuals) through evaluations. - Those who gained ranks through tribute or monetary offerings.
Higher-ranking San'i (fifth rank and above) stationed in the capital were assigned to the {{ill|San'i Bureau|ja|散位寮}}, while those of lower rank (sixth rank and below) either served at the San'i Bureau on a rotational basis if residing in the capital or at local {{ill|provincial offices|ja|国衙}} if based in the provinces. San'i could also receive secondary appointments to various offices, including {{ill|Ritsuryō-external institutions|ja|造寺司}}, or work under provincial governors as deputies ({{ill|Mokudai|ja|目代}}) or lower-ranking officials ({{ill|Zaichō Kanjin|ja|在庁官人}}). To address the growing number of San'i, a fixed quota system was introduced. Those exceeding the quota paid {{ill|Zokurō Coins|ja|続労銭}} to substitute for work obligations.
San'i status could also result from inadequate qualifications for a specific role, as prescribed under the {{ill|Senjo-ryō|ja|選叙令}} (Selection and Appointment Ordinance). Although not inherently a title for self-identification, it became a significant marker of status for local elites such as {{ill|Gunji|ja|郡司}} or {{ill|Tōne|ja|刀禰}}, who used their San'i rank in documentation to distinguish themselves from commoners and highlight their privileges, including tax exemptions.
== References == * Yamada Hideo, "San'i," in {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典}} 6 (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985), ISBN 978-4-642-00505-0. * Yamada Hideo, "San'i," in {{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten|ja|日本史大事典}} 3 (Heibonsha, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1. * Hori Noriko, "San'i," in {{ill|Nihon Kodai-shi Jiten|ja|日本古代史事典}} (Asakura Shoten, 2005), ISBN 978-4-254-53014-8. * Uemura Takashi, "Ancient Official Systems and the Role of 'Shoku'," in *Studies on the Formation of 'Shoku'* (Kōzō Shoin, 20pp), ISBN 978-4-7517-4360-7.
